image.title: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photoredox/Palladium Promoted Selective Dialkylation of α,α′‐Amino C(sp3)H Bonds from N,N‐Dimethyl‐1‐sulfonyl Enamines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We have established a facile and operationally simple protocol for the direct dialkylation of N,N‐dimethyl‐1‐sulfonyl enamines employing a dual photoredox and palladium catalytic system. The method excels in its compatibility with alkenyl retention, enabling the synthesis of structurally diverse, electron‐deficient 1‐sulfonyl‐2‐N,N‐dialkyl enamines with high functional group tolerance and perfect regioselectivity. The process is characterized by a stepwise dialkylation mechanism involving electronically neutral photoredox catalysis, with the second alkylation step highlighting the pivotal role of palladium catalyst.
Enamines serve as significant building blocks in α‐(asymmetric) functionalization of aldehydes and key intermediates in the direct deoxygenation of amides to β‐functionalized amines. However, examples on the selective alkylation of α‐amino C(sp3)H bonds of enamines are rare, and especially, the dialkylation of α,α′‐amino C(sp3)H bonds is still less explored. Herein, we report an acridinium–palladium‐catalyzed operationally simple method for the facile dialkylation of N,N‐dimethyl‐1‐sulfonyl enamines, which is particularly efficient for the alkenyl retention cases, providing diverse electron‐deficient N,N‐dialkyl‐1‐sulfonyl enamines with exceptional functional group tolerance and complete regiocontrol. Control experiments and detailed mechanism study reveal that the electron neutral photoredox catalysis proceeds through a step‐wise mechanism, and the radical palladium intermediate plays an important role in the second alkylation progress.
item.content:encoded:
We have established a facile and operationally simple protocol for the direct dialkylation of N,N-dimethyl-1-sulfonyl enamines employing a dual photoredox and palladium catalytic system. The method excels in its compatibility with alkenyl retention, enabling the synthesis of structurally diverse, electron-deficient 1-sulfonyl-2-N,N-dialkyl enamines with high functional group tolerance and perfect regioselectivity. The process is characterized by a stepwise dialkylation mechanism involving electronically neutral photoredox catalysis, with the second alkylation step highlighting the pivotal role of palladium catalyst.


Enamines serve as significant building blocks in α-(asymmetric) functionalization of aldehydes and key intermediates in the direct deoxygenation of amides to β-functionalized amines. However, examples on the selective alkylation of α-amino C(sp3)H bonds of enamines are rare, and especially, the dialkylation of α,α′-amino C(sp3)H bonds is still less explored. Herein, we report an acridinium–palladium-catalyzed operationally simple method for the facile dialkylation of N,N-dimethyl-1-sulfonyl enamines, which is particularly efficient for the alkenyl retention cases, providing diverse electron-deficient N,N-dialkyl-1-sulfonyl enamines with exceptional functional group tolerance and complete regiocontrol. Control experiments and detailed mechanism study reveal that the electron neutral photoredox catalysis proceeds through a step-wise mechanism, and the radical palladium intermediate plays an important role in the second alkylation progress.
item.dc:creator: Ankaier Tuersuntuoheti, Yidi Fan, Yuhao Yangzong, Yilanda Nihemaiti, Tingting Zhou, Takeshi Yamakawa, Jing Zhao, Leifeng Wang
item.dc:title: Photoredox/Palladium Promoted Selective Dialkylation of α,α′‐Amino C(sp3)H Bonds from N,N‐Dimethyl‐1‐sulfonyl Enamines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Effect of HMPA on the Ground and Excited State Reactivity of SmI2
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A tandem neural network directly solves the multivalued inverse problem of extracting semiconductor parameters from transistor measurements. Trained on only 1000 simulations, the network infers six material parameters (e.g., defect states, carrier concentration, mobility) in under 1 ms, demonstrating a broadly applicable framework for semiconductor analysis and enabling real‐time feedback for autonomous experimentation and digital twins.
Samarium Diiodide (SmI2) is a privileged reducing agent in organic synthesis. Tunability of reactivity and selectivity of SmI2 through additives made this reagent an attractive choice for organic chemists. Present investigation showed contrasting effects of hexamethylphosphoramide (HMPA) on the ground and excited states of reactions of SmI2 with haloalkanes. While HMPA enhances the rate of reduction of haloalkanes in the ground state, a deleterious impact was found for excited‐state reactions. Detailed investigations with kinetics, element effect, known BDE of CH bonds, and computed CX bond distance indicated that while ground state reactivity is governed by stability of radicals and weakening of CX bond due to HMPA and SmI2 coordination, excited state reactions are predominantly governed by inner sphere nature of electron transfer. The critical role of HMPA in facilitating the reduction of haloalkanes was confirmed using density functional theory‐based calculations.
item.content:encoded:
A tandem neural network directly solves the multivalued inverse problem of extracting semiconductor parameters from transistor measurements. Trained on only 1000 simulations, the network infers six material parameters (e.g., defect states, carrier concentration, mobility) in under 1 ms, demonstrating a broadly applicable framework for semiconductor analysis and enabling real-time feedback for autonomous experimentation and digital twins.


Samarium Diiodide (SmI2) is a privileged reducing agent in organic synthesis. Tunability of reactivity and selectivity of SmI2 through additives made this reagent an attractive choice for organic chemists. Present investigation showed contrasting effects of hexamethylphosphoramide (HMPA) on the ground and excited states of reactions of SmI2 with haloalkanes. While HMPA enhances the rate of reduction of haloalkanes in the ground state, a deleterious impact was found for excited-state reactions. Detailed investigations with kinetics, element effect, known BDE of CH bonds, and computed CX bond distance indicated that while ground state reactivity is governed by stability of radicals and weakening of CX bond due to HMPA and SmI2 coordination, excited state reactions are predominantly governed by inner sphere nature of electron transfer. The critical role of HMPA in facilitating the reduction of haloalkanes was confirmed using density functional theory-based calculations.
item.dc:creator: Suranjan De, Athira Jayasankar, Sooraj Kunnikuruvan, Sandeepan Maity
item.dc:title: Effect of HMPA on the Ground and Excited State Reactivity of SmI2

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Palladium‐Catalyzed [3 + 2] Annulation of Spirovinylcyclopropyl Oxindoles: A Ring‐Opening/Annulation Cascade to 1,3‐Dispirocyclopentane Skeletons
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A palladium‐catalyzed cascade reaction has been developed for the construction of dispiro cyclopentane‐1,2’‐indanedione‐3,3’’‐indoline frameworks. 21 examples of dispiro derivatives were prepared. This method features mild conditions, simple operation, facile scalability, and broad substrate compatibility.
A palladium‐catalyzed cascade reaction has been developed for the construction of dispiro cyclopentane‐1,2’‐indanedione‐3,3’’‐indoline frameworks. This strategy resorts to the unique reactivity of stable spirovinylcyclopropyl oxindoles and 2‐arylidene‐1,3‐indanediones, enabling a ring‐opening followed by a [3 + 2] annulation in a single operation. The protocol is distinguished by its mild conditions, high efficiency, as well as broad substrate generality, providing a versatile access to these valuable molecular architectures.
item.content:encoded:
A palladium-catalyzed cascade reaction has been developed for the construction of dispiro cyclopentane-1,2’-indanedione-3,3’’-indoline frameworks. 21 examples of dispiro derivatives were prepared. This method features mild conditions, simple operation, facile scalability, and broad substrate compatibility.


A palladium-catalyzed cascade reaction has been developed for the construction of dispiro cyclopentane-1,2’-indanedione-3,3’’-indoline frameworks. This strategy resorts to the unique reactivity of stable spirovinylcyclopropyl oxindoles and 2-arylidene-1,3-indanediones, enabling a ring-opening followed by a [3 + 2] annulation in a single operation. The protocol is distinguished by its mild conditions, high efficiency, as well as broad substrate generality, providing a versatile access to these valuable molecular architectures.
item.dc:creator: Yingpeng Su, Fang Shi, Guiyan Cao, Haiyang He, Shufang Zhang, Miao Xiang, Yuntong Lu, Xiaolong Zhao, Congde Huo, Xiaolei Wang
item.dc:title: Palladium‐Catalyzed [3 + 2] Annulation of Spirovinylcyclopropyl Oxindoles: A Ring‐Opening/Annulation Cascade to 1,3‐Dispirocyclopentane Skeletons

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Advances in CN Condensation Strategies for Benzimidazole Synthesis: A Synthetic Methodology Review
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Benzimidazoles are well known structural motifs that are used in material sciences, medicines, and pharmaceuticals. They also act as an important class of heterocyclic species for the transformation into various useful molecules as a complement to previous protocols of CH functionalization for the synthesis and application of benzimidazoles. Therefore, this review is targeted to the direct synthesis, mechanism and CH functionalization of benzimidazoles using various transition metal catalyst. Moreover, hypervalent iodine, radical‐ mediated and metal‐free approaches were also highlighted.
Ongoing advances in synthetic methods have greatly expanded access to benzimidazole derivatives through innovative catalytic and non‐catalytic approaches. This review focuses on recent advances in copper‐catalyzed reactions, condensation processes, noble‐metal, and first‐row transition‐metal catalysis, as well as metal‐free, hypervalent iodine‐mediated, and radical‐based techniques for constructing benzimidazole scaffolds. This review highlights significant contributions to mechanistic insights, catalyst systems, and substrate scope, highlighting methods that enable one‐pot transformations, direct CH functionalization, and aiming to provide a conceptual summary that will be useful to researchers and inspire further developments in the rapidly growing medicinal field and advanced synthetic design.
item.content:encoded:
Benzimidazoles are well known structural motifs that are used in material sciences, medicines, and pharmaceuticals. They also act as an important class of heterocyclic species for the transformation into various useful molecules as a complement to previous protocols of CH functionalization for the synthesis and application of benzimidazoles. Therefore, this review is targeted to the direct synthesis, mechanism and CH functionalization of benzimidazoles using various transition metal catalyst. Moreover, hypervalent iodine, radical- mediated and metal-free approaches were also highlighted.


Ongoing advances in synthetic methods have greatly expanded access to benzimidazole derivatives through innovative catalytic and non-catalytic approaches. This review focuses on recent advances in copper-catalyzed reactions, condensation processes, noble-metal, and first-row transition-metal catalysis, as well as metal-free, hypervalent iodine-mediated, and radical-based techniques for constructing benzimidazole scaffolds. This review highlights significant contributions to mechanistic insights, catalyst systems, and substrate scope, highlighting methods that enable one-pot transformations, direct CH functionalization, and aiming to provide a conceptual summary that will be useful to researchers and inspire further developments in the rapidly growing medicinal field and advanced synthetic design.
item.dc:creator: Nidhi, Laxmi Yadav, Arpit Srivastav, Abhishek Srivastava, Neeraj Kumar Mishra, Vinay Kumar Singh
item.dc:title: Advances in CN Condensation Strategies for Benzimidazole Synthesis: A Synthetic Methodology Review

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of Polysubstituted Furans via a [3+2] Cycloaddition Reaction of β‐Ketosulfones With Styrenes Under Copper Catalysis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Herein, we demonstrate that the reaction of β‐ketosulfone with p‐methoxystyrene in DMSO at 120°C under air conditions for 12 h can effectively produce a series of furan derivatives, with a maximum yield of 84%. A total of 27 target compounds were obtained.
Herein, we demonstrate that the reaction of β‐ketosulfone with p‐methoxystyrene in DMSO at 120°C under air conditions for 12 h can effectively produce a series of furan derivatives, with a maximum yield of 84%. A total of 27 target compounds were obtained, demonstrating a wide range of substrate applicability and excellent functional group tolerance, thus offering a straightforward and effective method for synthesizing furan derivatives.
item.content:encoded:
Herein, we demonstrate that the reaction of β-ketosulfone with p-methoxystyrene in DMSO at 120°C under air conditions for 12 h can effectively produce a series of furan derivatives, with a maximum yield of 84%. A total of 27 target compounds were obtained.


Herein, we demonstrate that the reaction of β-ketosulfone with p-methoxystyrene in DMSO at 120°C under air conditions for 12 h can effectively produce a series of furan derivatives, with a maximum yield of 84%. A total of 27 target compounds were obtained, demonstrating a wide range of substrate applicability and excellent functional group tolerance, thus offering a straightforward and effective method for synthesizing furan derivatives.
item.dc:creator: Guirong You, Kaiqi Wang, Jian Sun, Rui Xu, Chengcai Xia, Liqiang Hao
item.dc:title: Synthesis of Polysubstituted Furans via a [3+2] Cycloaddition Reaction of β‐Ketosulfones With Styrenes Under Copper Catalysis

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Stereoselective Synthesis of Thiocyanato/Selenocyanato‐Containing Z‐Vinyl Sulfides Via Tf2O/DMSO‐Promoted Sulfenofunctionalization of Alkynes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A practical sulfonium salt‐mediated regio‐ and stereoselective thio/selenocyanation of alkynes using inexpensive NH4SCN and KSeCN as the thiocyanating and selenocyanating agents has been readily developed through a one‐pot, two‐step process under simple conditions.
In this work, we developed a practical sulfonium‐salt mediated difunctionalization of alkynes with NH4SCN/KSeCN. This method enables the effective synthesis of various thiocyanato/selenocyanato (SCN/SeCN)‐containing vinyl sulfides using DMSO as a readily available methylthio source. The reaction exhibits excellent regio‐ and Z‐selectivity with a broad substrate scope and high functional group compatibility. Gram‐scale experiments and late‐stage functionalization further make this protocol quite attractive in organic synthesis.
item.content:encoded:
A practical sulfonium salt-mediated regio- and stereoselective thio/selenocyanation of alkynes using inexpensive NH4SCN and KSeCN as the thiocyanating and selenocyanating agents has been readily developed through a one-pot, two-step process under simple conditions.


In this work, we developed a practical sulfonium-salt mediated difunctionalization of alkynes with NH4SCN/KSeCN. This method enables the effective synthesis of various thiocyanato/selenocyanato (SCN/SeCN)-containing vinyl sulfides using DMSO as a readily available methylthio source. The reaction exhibits excellent regio- and Z-selectivity with a broad substrate scope and high functional group compatibility. Gram-scale experiments and late-stage functionalization further make this protocol quite attractive in organic synthesis.
item.dc:creator: Zheng‐Wu Tan, Hong Yang, Xiang‐Qin Xu, Li‐Hua Yang, Long‐Yong Xie
item.dc:title: Stereoselective Synthesis of Thiocyanato/Selenocyanato‐Containing Z‐Vinyl Sulfides Via Tf2O/DMSO‐Promoted Sulfenofunctionalization of Alkynes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Regio‐ and Stereoselective Iodochlorination/Cross‐Coupling Strategy for Isomeric Trifluoromethylated Tetrasubstituted Alkenes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Trifluoromethylated tetrasubstituted alkenes are valuable motifs but challenging to synthesize stereoselectively. Here, we report a regio‐ and stereoselective iodochlorination of trifluoromethylated alkynes, providing versatile dihalides. Sequential cross‐coupling reactions enabled access to distinct isomers, offering a general strategy for the synthesis of complex trifluoromethylated alkenes for pharmaceutical and materials applications.
Trifluoromethylated tetrasubstituted alkenes are valuable motifs in biologically active compounds and functional materials, but their stereoselective synthesis remains challenging owing to the limited number of methods available. In this study, we describe a highly regio‐ and stereoselective iodochlorination of trifluoromethylated internal alkynes using N‐iodosuccinimide and lithium chloride in an acetic acid/tetrahydrofuran mixed solvent system. The resulting vicinal dihalides serve as versatile intermediates for sequential cross‐coupling reactions, thereby providing efficient and stereodefined routes to densely substituted alkenes. Importantly, judicious choice of both substrates and coupling partners enables the selective preparation of distinct alkene isomers, demonstrating the method's flexibility. This approach offers a straightforward and general strategy for constructing complex trifluoromethylated alkenes, with potential applications in medicinal chemistry, agrochemicals, and materials science, where precise control of alkene geometry is essential.
item.content:encoded:
Trifluoromethylated tetrasubstituted alkenes are valuable motifs but challenging to synthesize stereoselectively. Here, we report a regio- and stereoselective iodochlorination of trifluoromethylated alkynes, providing versatile dihalides. Sequential cross-coupling reactions enabled access to distinct isomers, offering a general strategy for the synthesis of complex trifluoromethylated alkenes for pharmaceutical and materials applications.


Trifluoromethylated tetrasubstituted alkenes are valuable motifs in biologically active compounds and functional materials, but their stereoselective synthesis remains challenging owing to the limited number of methods available. In this study, we describe a highly regio- and stereoselective iodochlorination of trifluoromethylated internal alkynes using N-iodosuccinimide and lithium chloride in an acetic acid/tetrahydrofuran mixed solvent system. The resulting vicinal dihalides serve as versatile intermediates for sequential cross-coupling reactions, thereby providing efficient and stereodefined routes to densely substituted alkenes. Importantly, judicious choice of both substrates and coupling partners enables the selective preparation of distinct alkene isomers, demonstrating the method's flexibility. This approach offers a straightforward and general strategy for constructing complex trifluoromethylated alkenes, with potential applications in medicinal chemistry, agrochemicals, and materials science, where precise control of alkene geometry is essential.
item.dc:creator: Taiyo Saito, Yuta Kabumoto, Yudai Yoshioka, Tatsuya Kumon, Kaho Kasai, Motohiro Yasui, Shigeyuki Yamada, Kenji Kanaori, Tsutomu Konno
item.dc:title: Regio‐ and Stereoselective Iodochlorination/Cross‐Coupling Strategy for Isomeric Trifluoromethylated Tetrasubstituted Alkenes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Ru(II)‐Catalyzed Regioselective C8–H Sulfonamidation of Isoquinolones With Sulfonyl Azides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We report a Ru(II)‐catalyzed, directing‐group‐assisted regioselective C8–H sulfonamidation of N‐aryl and N‐alkylisoquinolones using sulfonyl azides. The transformation proceeds under mild conditions with excellent regioselectivity. Aryl and polyaromatic sulfonyl azides delivered products in good to excellent yields. This protocol provides access to pharmaceutically relevant C8‐sulfonamidated isoquinolones.
We report a Ru(II)‐catalyzed, directing‐group‐assisted regioselective C8–H sulfonamidation of N‐aryl and N‐alkylisoquinolones using sulfonyl azides. The transformation proceeds under mild conditions with excellent regioselectivity. Aryl and polyaromatic sulfonyl azides delivered products in good to excellent yields. This protocol affords access to pharmaceutically relevant C8‐sulfonamidated isoquinolones.
item.content:encoded:
We report a Ru(II)-catalyzed, directing-group-assisted regioselective C8–H sulfonamidation of N-aryl and N-alkylisoquinolones using sulfonyl azides. The transformation proceeds under mild conditions with excellent regioselectivity. Aryl and polyaromatic sulfonyl azides delivered products in good to excellent yields. This protocol provides access to pharmaceutically relevant C8-sulfonamidated isoquinolones.


We report a Ru(II)-catalyzed, directing-group-assisted regioselective C8–H sulfonamidation of N-aryl and N-alkylisoquinolones using sulfonyl azides. The transformation proceeds under mild conditions with excellent regioselectivity. Aryl and polyaromatic sulfonyl azides delivered products in good to excellent yields. This protocol affords access to pharmaceutically relevant C8-sulfonamidated isoquinolones.
item.dc:creator: Suruchi Kumari, Abhishek Kumar Shaw, Anushka Rastogi, Ruchir Kant, Dipankar Koley
item.dc:title: Ru(II)‐Catalyzed Regioselective C8–H Sulfonamidation of Isoquinolones With Sulfonyl Azides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: The Rapid Construction and Biological Evaluation of Densely Substituted Carbazoles Via I2/H2O‐Mediated Green Approach
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A greener catalytic route to carbazoles bridges sustainable synthesis with biological activity.
We report a green and efficient iodine‐catalysed cascade reaction of indoles with α‐bromoacetophenones, enabling the rapid and highly regioselective construction of diverse carbazole scaffolds. Remarkably, this metal‐ and oxidant‐free protocol operates with only 5 mol% iodine in water, converting unprotected and protected indoles to carbazoles in excellent yields. A series of carbazole derivatives were synthesised under mild, operationally simple conditions, demonstrating broad functional group tolerance and excellent scalability to gram‐scale synthesis. The biological evaluation revealed that among the compounds tested, six compounds showed cell viability below 50% in A549 lung cancer cells and whereas nine compounds in SKOV3 ovarian cancer cells. Selectivity index calculations further indicated that one compound displayed moderate selectivity towards A549 cells, whereas five compounds showed preferential cytotoxicity against SKOV3 cells, highlighting the promising therapeutic potential of these scaffolds. Collectively, these findings underscore the medicinal relevance of the synthesised carbazole frameworks and the potential of this cost‐effective, chemo‐divergent and sustainable strategy for generating structurally complex, bioactive carbazole cores in anti‐cancer drug development.
item.content:encoded:
A greener catalytic route to carbazoles bridges sustainable synthesis with biological activity.


We report a green and efficient iodine-catalysed cascade reaction of indoles with α-bromoacetophenones, enabling the rapid and highly regioselective construction of diverse carbazole scaffolds. Remarkably, this metal- and oxidant-free protocol operates with only 5 mol% iodine in water, converting unprotected and protected indoles to carbazoles in excellent yields. A series of carbazole derivatives were synthesised under mild, operationally simple conditions, demonstrating broad functional group tolerance and excellent scalability to gram-scale synthesis. The biological evaluation revealed that among the compounds tested, six compounds showed cell viability below 50% in A549 lung cancer cells and whereas nine compounds in SKOV3 ovarian cancer cells. Selectivity index calculations further indicated that one compound displayed moderate selectivity towards A549 cells, whereas five compounds showed preferential cytotoxicity against SKOV3 cells, highlighting the promising therapeutic potential of these scaffolds. Collectively, these findings underscore the medicinal relevance of the synthesised carbazole frameworks and the potential of this cost-effective, chemo-divergent and sustainable strategy for generating structurally complex, bioactive carbazole cores in anti-cancer drug development.
item.dc:creator: Amar Nath Singh Chauhan, Kailas Arjun Chavan, Swaranjali D. Gajare, Supriya Sharma, Anjali Bhujbal, Omkar S. Dalvi, Netra Hiremath, Rishabh Kumar Srivastava, Akhilesh Kumar, Raviraj Vankayala, Rohan D. Erande
item.dc:title: The Rapid Construction and Biological Evaluation of Densely Substituted Carbazoles Via I2/H2O‐Mediated Green Approach

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: A Formal Nitrogen Atom Insertion into N‐Aroyl‐7‐Azaindoles Toward Blocked Isocyanates for the Synthesis of Unsymmetrical Ureas
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
N‐Phenyl‐1H‐pyrrolo[2,3‐b]pyridine‐1‐carboxamides, a new class of blocked isocyanates with balanced stability and reactivity, were synthesized by the reaction of TMSN3 with N‐aroyl‐7‐azaindoles. Transamidation of these carboxamides with various amines was successfully achieved to produce unsymmetrical ureas.
The interaction of TMSN3 with N‐aroyl‐7‐azaindoles in the presence of an acid catalyst resulted in a formal nitrogen atom insertion, leading to the synthesis of N‐phenyl‐1H‐pyrrolo[2,3‐b]pyridine‐1‐carboxamides, a novel class of blocked isocyanates. Mechanistic investigations demonstrated that the key step in the reaction was the Curtius rearrangement, which involves a sequence of elimination and addition processes of the 7‐azaindole moiety. Furthermore, transamidation of these carboxamides with various amines was successfully conducted to produce unsymmetrical ureas.
item.content:encoded:
N-Phenyl-1H-pyrrolo[2,3-b]pyridine-1-carboxamides, a new class of blocked isocyanates with balanced stability and reactivity, were synthesized by the reaction of TMSN3 with N-aroyl-7-azaindoles. Transamidation of these carboxamides with various amines was successfully achieved to produce unsymmetrical ureas.


The interaction of TMSN3 with N-aroyl-7-azaindoles in the presence of an acid catalyst resulted in a formal nitrogen atom insertion, leading to the synthesis of N-phenyl-1H-pyrrolo[2,3-b]pyridine-1-carboxamides, a novel class of blocked isocyanates. Mechanistic investigations demonstrated that the key step in the reaction was the Curtius rearrangement, which involves a sequence of elimination and addition processes of the 7-azaindole moiety. Furthermore, transamidation of these carboxamides with various amines was successfully conducted to produce unsymmetrical ureas.
item.dc:creator: Mei Zhang, Meng‐Meng Xu, Yu‐Xiang Wu, Yong‐Jun Yuan, Shun‐Jun Ji, Xiao‐Ping Xu
item.dc:title: A Formal Nitrogen Atom Insertion into N‐Aroyl‐7‐Azaindoles Toward Blocked Isocyanates for the Synthesis of Unsymmetrical Ureas

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Mechanistic and Reactivity Insights into Diboron‐Mediated NN and NN Bond Scission
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review highlights the emerging ability of diboron reagents to cleave both π‐bonded (N=N) and σ‐bonded (N—N) nitrogen–nitrogen linkages, integrating mechanistic understanding with recent synthetic applications.
Diboron reagents have emerged as versatile reducing agents capable of transforming a broad range of functional groups through pathways like hydrogen‐ or metal–hydride‐based reductions. Moreover, the strong thermodynamic driving force for BO bond formation enables efficient cleavage of XO bonds and straightforward deoxygenation without the need for external hydrogen sources such as water or alcohol. This intrinsic reactivity can be further enhanced by bases or Lewis‐basic additives, including pyridines, which promote BB bond activation and broaden the scope of transformations accessible under mild conditions. The effectiveness of diboron‐mediated XO bond cleavage naturally raises the question of whether these reagents might also promote the scission of NN bondsa transformation of increasing interest due to the prevalence of NN motifs in synthetic intermediates, pharmaceuticals, and nitrogen‐rich frameworks. This review outlines current understanding of diboron‐mediated NN and NN bond cleavage and unifies mechanistic and reactivity insights to guide future advances in diboron‐based synthetic transformations.
item.content:encoded:
This review highlights the emerging ability of diboron reagents to cleave both π-bonded (N=N) and σ-bonded (N—N) nitrogen–nitrogen linkages, integrating mechanistic understanding with recent synthetic applications.


Diboron reagents have emerged as versatile reducing agents capable of transforming a broad range of functional groups through pathways like hydrogen- or metal–hydride-based reductions. Moreover, the strong thermodynamic driving force for BO bond formation enables efficient cleavage of XO bonds and straightforward deoxygenation without the need for external hydrogen sources such as water or alcohol. This intrinsic reactivity can be further enhanced by bases or Lewis-basic additives, including pyridines, which promote BB bond activation and broaden the scope of transformations accessible under mild conditions. The effectiveness of diboron-mediated XO bond cleavage naturally raises the question of whether these reagents might also promote the scission of NN bondsa transformation of increasing interest due to the prevalence of NN motifs in synthetic intermediates, pharmaceuticals, and nitrogen-rich frameworks. This review outlines current understanding of diboron-mediated NN and NN bond cleavage and unifies mechanistic and reactivity insights to guide future advances in diboron-based synthetic transformations.
item.dc:creator: Raúl Valderrama‐Callejón, Inés Alonso, M. Belén Cid
item.dc:title: Mechanistic and Reactivity Insights into Diboron‐Mediated NN and NN Bond Scission

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Induced Chain Elongation Reaction of Tetrahydrofuran for the Synthesis of Cinnamate Esters
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 20, 27 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Visible‐light‐induced three‐component reactions of cinnamic acid, diazoesters, and tetrahydrofuran/acetonitrile have been developed for the synthesis of cinnamic acid esters. The synthetic methods, which are performed under mild conditions, feature broad substrate scope and functional group tolerance.
A visible‐light‐induced reaction of cinnamic acid and diazoester is developed. In the presence of THF, this reaction provided chain elongated cinnamic acid esters via formation and ring opening of oxonium ylide with cinnamic acid. The same light‐induced reaction of cinnamic acid and diazoester in MeCN provided simple esters of cinnamic acid.
item.content:encoded:
Visible-light-induced three-component reactions of cinnamic acid, diazoesters, and tetrahydrofuran/acetonitrile have been developed for the synthesis of cinnamic acid esters. The synthetic methods, which are performed under mild conditions, feature broad substrate scope and functional group tolerance.


A visible-light-induced reaction of cinnamic acid and diazoester is developed. In the presence of THF, this reaction provided chain elongated cinnamic acid esters via formation and ring opening of oxonium ylide with cinnamic acid. The same light-induced reaction of cinnamic acid and diazoester in MeCN provided simple esters of cinnamic acid.
item.dc:creator: Deep J. Kalita, Rajtonoy Bharali, Srinivas Ambala, Sanjib Gogoi
item.dc:title: Visible‐Light‐Induced Chain Elongation Reaction of Tetrahydrofuran for the Synthesis of Cinnamate Esters

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of Homopropargyl Alcohols via Zinc Iodide Mediated Three‐Component Reaction of Propargyl Carbamates With Organoboranes and Aldehydes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 20, 27 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
This study presents a novel and practical method for synthesizing homopropargyl alcohols via a zinc iodide‐mediated three‐component reaction of terminal alkyne‐bearing propargyl carbamates, trialkylboranes, and aldehydes under mild reaction conditions. A wide range of α,γ‐substituted allenylboranes are formed in situ through a 1,2‐metallate shift of alkynylborates to the sp carbon atom.
Homopropargyl alcohols are versatile building blocks in a wide range of synthetic transformations; consequently, the development of efficient and practical methods for their preparation remains an important objective in organic synthesis. This study presents a novel and practical zinc iodide mediated three‐component reaction of terminal alkyne‐bearing propargyl carbamates, trialkylboranes, and aldehydes for the synthesis of homopropargyl alcohols. A variety of α,γ‐substituted allenylboranes are formed in situ through a 1,2‐metallate shift of alkynylborates to the sp carbon atom, which subsequently react with aldehydes to afford the corresponding homopropargyl alcohols. In contrast to conventional methods for the generation of alkynylborates, the present system requires only a catalytic amount of lithium diisopropylamide as the base.
item.content:encoded:
This study presents a novel and practical method for synthesizing homopropargyl alcohols via a zinc iodide-mediated three-component reaction of terminal alkyne-bearing propargyl carbamates, trialkylboranes, and aldehydes under mild reaction conditions. A wide range of α,γ-substituted allenylboranes are formed in situ through a 1,2-metallate shift of alkynylborates to the sp carbon atom.


Homopropargyl alcohols are versatile building blocks in a wide range of synthetic transformations; consequently, the development of efficient and practical methods for their preparation remains an important objective in organic synthesis. This study presents a novel and practical zinc iodide mediated three-component reaction of terminal alkyne-bearing propargyl carbamates, trialkylboranes, and aldehydes for the synthesis of homopropargyl alcohols. A variety of α,γ-substituted allenylboranes are formed in situ through a 1,2-metallate shift of alkynylborates to the sp carbon atom, which subsequently react with aldehydes to afford the corresponding homopropargyl alcohols. In contrast to conventional methods for the generation of alkynylborates, the present system requires only a catalytic amount of lithium diisopropylamide as the base.

item.title: Zwitterionic C–N Bond Cleavage by the Accumulation of Charge: Green Synthesis of Amino Thiazolyl Benzimidazolium Bromide and Benzimidazol‐Thiazole under Microwave Irradiations
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 20, 27 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
The reaction was carried out for the C–N bond cleavage by the accumulation of charge. The zwitterionic intermediate stabilized by cyclic hydrogen bonding. The accumulation of positive charge at lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and accumulation of negative charge at highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) decreases the HOMO‐LUMO band gap and resulted to C–N bond cleavage. L‐proline was used as organocatalyst under microwave irradiations.
The Zwitterionic curious methodology for the regioselective C–N bond cleavage to synthesize the anticancer substituted amino thiazolyl benzimidazolium bromide and its thiazole. The cyclic hydrogen bonding (multiple hydrogen bonding) approach with accumulation of charge have been applied for the regioselective C–N bond cleavage by organocatalysis. It is metal free one‐pot reaction fascinated by microwave irradiations. The zwitterionic L‐proline have been proposed as catalyst and acceptor partner in donor–acceptor cyclic hydrogen bonding system. The work is green and sustainable without separation, purification and solvent extraction. The respective accumulation of positive charge and negative charge at lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) decreases energy gap in cyclic hydrogen bonding. The accumulation of positive charge on two carbons executed the C–N bond cleavage effectively by the migration of benzimidazole moiety. The density functional theory is used to study the intermediates using HOMO‐LUMO frontier orbital concept. This elite cyclic hydrogen bonding between bromide salt and neutral thiazole moiety abetted the solidification of product in solution. The synthesized products are anticancer agents. All products created new library of amino thiazolyl benzimidazolium bromide and their thiazole heterocycles.
item.content:encoded:

The accumulation of positive charge at lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and accumulation of negative charge at highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) decreases the HOMO-LUMO band gap and resulted to C–N bond cleavage. L-proline was used as organocatalyst under microwave irradiations.


The Zwitterionic curious methodology for the regioselective C–N bond cleavage to synthesize the anticancer substituted amino thiazolyl benzimidazolium bromide and its thiazole. The cyclic hydrogen bonding (multiple hydrogen bonding) approach with accumulation of charge have been applied for the regioselective C–N bond cleavage by organocatalysis. It is metal free one-pot reaction fascinated by microwave irradiations. The zwitterionic L-proline have been proposed as catalyst and acceptor partner in donor–acceptor cyclic hydrogen bonding system. The work is green and sustainable without separation, purification and solvent extraction. The respective accumulation of positive charge and negative charge at lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) decreases energy gap in cyclic hydrogen bonding. The accumulation of positive charge on two carbons executed the C–N bond cleavage effectively by the migration of benzimidazole moiety. The density functional theory is used to study the intermediates using HOMO-LUMO frontier orbital concept. This elite cyclic hydrogen bonding between bromide salt and neutral thiazole moiety abetted the solidification of product in solution. The synthesized products are anticancer agents. All products created new library of amino thiazolyl benzimidazolium bromide and their thiazole heterocycles.

item.title: Visible‐Light‐Catalyzed Ring‐Opening Halogenation of Spiro‐Dihydroquinazolinones Using Perhalogenated Methanes as Halogen Sources
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 20, 27 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A cascade reaction involving spiro C—C σ–bond cleavage and C–halogen bond formation is reported for the efficient construction of bromo‐/chloroalkyl‐substituted quinazolin‐4(3H)‐ones from spiro‐dihydroquinazolinones and perhalogenated methanes under organophotoredox catalysis.
Visible‐light‐catalyzed aromatization‐driven deconstructive bromination and chlorination of spiro‐dihydroquinazolinones are described, with perhalogenated methanes serving as halogenating agents. A series of remotely and site‐specifically bromoalkyl‐ and chloroalkyl‐substituted quinazolin‐4(3H)‐ones were obtained with moderate yields and functional‐group tolerance. A radical pathway involving nitrogen‐centered‐radical‐induced C─C bond cleavage and carbon–halogen bond formation is proposed for this transformation.
item.content:encoded:
A cascade reaction involving spiro C—C σ–bond cleavage and C–halogen bond formation is reported for the efficient construction of bromo-/chloroalkyl-substituted quinazolin-4(3H)-ones from spiro-dihydroquinazolinones and perhalogenated methanes under organophotoredox catalysis.


Visible-light-catalyzed aromatization-driven deconstructive bromination and chlorination of spiro-dihydroquinazolinones are described, with perhalogenated methanes serving as halogenating agents. A series of remotely and site-specifically bromoalkyl- and chloroalkyl-substituted quinazolin-4(3H)-ones were obtained with moderate yields and functional-group tolerance. A radical pathway involving nitrogen-centered-radical-induced C─C bond cleavage and carbon–halogen bond formation is proposed for this transformation.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Versatile Synthesis of Δ9‐Tetrahydrocannabinol and Related Compounds by Protecting‐Group‐Free Suzuki–Miyaura Coupling
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 20, 27 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
An operationally simple method for synthesizing Δ9‐tetrahydrocannabinol (Δ9‐THC) and its analogs via Suzuki–Miyaura coupling provides rapid access to diverse Δ9‐THC analogs, with potential applications for affording authentic standards of suspect compounds in forensic contexts with limited facilities.
Δ9‐Tetrahydrocannabinol (Δ9‐THC) was synthesized via Suzuki–Miyaura coupling of a triflate precursor, derived from an inexpensive terpenoid and phloroglucinol, with a boronic acid derivative. This method also provides easy access to Δ9‐THC derivatives bearing a variety of n‐alkyl, aryl and alkenyl side chains and is applicable to other cannabinoids, including cannabidiol and Δ8‐THC derivatives, without requiring strictly anhydrous conditions, special equipment, or protection of the phenolic hydroxyl group. It is expected to be useful in preparing authentic standards of suspected illicit drugs in forensic laboratories where expertise and facilities for organic synthesis may be limited.
item.content:encoded:
An operationally simple method for synthesizing Δ9-tetrahydrocannabinol (Δ9-THC) and its analogs via Suzuki–Miyaura coupling provides rapid access to diverse Δ9-THC analogs, with potential applications for affording authentic standards of suspect compounds in forensic contexts with limited facilities.


Δ9-Tetrahydrocannabinol (Δ9-THC) was synthesized via Suzuki–Miyaura coupling of a triflate precursor, derived from an inexpensive terpenoid and phloroglucinol, with a boronic acid derivative. This method also provides easy access to Δ9-THC derivatives bearing a variety of n-alkyl, aryl and alkenyl side chains and is applicable to other cannabinoids, including cannabidiol and Δ8-THC derivatives, without requiring strictly anhydrous conditions, special equipment, or protection of the phenolic hydroxyl group. It is expected to be useful in preparing authentic standards of suspected illicit drugs in forensic laboratories where expertise and facilities for organic synthesis may be limited.

item.dc:title: Versatile Synthesis of Δ9‐Tetrahydrocannabinol and Related Compounds by Protecting‐Group‐Free Suzuki–Miyaura Coupling

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Metal‐Free and Solvent‐Free CO2 Fixation Into Cyclic Carbonates Catalyzed by a Deferiprone‐Derived Bifunctional System
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 20, 27 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A simple bifunctional organocatalyst derived from the FDA‐approved chelator deferiprone efficiently promotes CO2 fixation into cyclic carbonates. By combining epoxide activation and nucleophilic ring opening in a single scaffold, quantitative conversions are achieved under mild, metal‐free conditions. High activity, broad substrate scope, and excellent recyclability highlight a sustainable alternative to conventional catalysts.
The chemical fixation of CO2 into cyclic carbonates represents a sustainable approach for carbon utilization. In this study, a series of bifunctional ammonium chelating catalysts (CA1–CA5) was synthesized and evaluated for their ability to catalyze the coupling of epoxides with CO2. The design aimed to integrate a catechol‐like portion for epoxide activation with an ammonium halide for ring opening. Among the screened candidates, CA1, derived from the simple salification of the FDA‐approved iron chelator deferiprone, emerged as the most effective and synthetically accessible catalyst. Optimization studies demonstrated that the bromide salt (CA1Br) significantly outperforms its chloride counterpart, achieving 100% conversion of styrene oxide under mild conditions (70°C, 4 bar CO2, 6 h) at a 1:5 molar ratio. CA1Br showed higher activity than the commercial catalyst tetrabutylammonium bromide (87% conversion) and proved versatile across a broad range of 12 substrates, particularly those that stabilize incipient carbocations. Furthermore, the catalyst exhibited excellent reusability, maintaining high performance for up to five cycles and allowing easy separation from the reaction mixture. Given its metal‐free nature, mild operating conditions, and sustainable precursor, CA1Br represents a competitive and eco‐friendly alternative to current industrial catalysts for CO2 fixation.
item.content:encoded:
A simple bifunctional organocatalyst derived from the FDA-approved chelator deferiprone efficiently promotes CO2 fixation into cyclic carbonates. By combining epoxide activation and nucleophilic ring opening in a single scaffold, quantitative conversions are achieved under mild, metal-free conditions. High activity, broad substrate scope, and excellent recyclability highlight a sustainable alternative to conventional catalysts.


The chemical fixation of CO2 into cyclic carbonates represents a sustainable approach for carbon utilization. In this study, a series of bifunctional ammonium chelating catalysts (CA1–CA5) was synthesized and evaluated for their ability to catalyze the coupling of epoxides with CO2. The design aimed to integrate a catechol-like portion for epoxide activation with an ammonium halide for ring opening. Among the screened candidates, CA1, derived from the simple salification of the FDA-approved iron chelator deferiprone, emerged as the most effective and synthetically accessible catalyst. Optimization studies demonstrated that the bromide salt (CA1Br) significantly outperforms its chloride counterpart, achieving 100% conversion of styrene oxide under mild conditions (70°C, 4 bar CO2, 6 h) at a 1:5 molar ratio. CA1Br showed higher activity than the commercial catalyst tetrabutylammonium bromide (87% conversion) and proved versatile across a broad range of 12 substrates, particularly those that stabilize incipient carbocations. Furthermore, the catalyst exhibited excellent reusability, maintaining high performance for up to five cycles and allowing easy separation from the reaction mixture. Given its metal-free nature, mild operating conditions, and sustainable precursor, CA1Br represents a competitive and eco-friendly alternative to current industrial catalysts for CO2 fixation.
item.dc:creator: Erika Saccullo, Luca Calantropo, Roberto Fiorenza, Pietro Nicotra, Antonio Rescifina, Vincenzo Patamia, Giuseppe Floresta
item.dc:title: Metal‐Free and Solvent‐Free CO2 Fixation Into Cyclic Carbonates Catalyzed by a Deferiprone‐Derived Bifunctional System

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photochemical Decatungstate‐Catalyzed Hydroacylation of Maleimides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 20, 27 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A photocatalytic protocol to access substituted succinimides via tetra‐n‐butylammonium decatungstate photocatalysis is reported. This direct radical‐driven hydroacylation of a variety of N‐substituted maleimides shows tolerance to various aldehydes (or alkanes), affording differentially substituted succinimides in high yields.
Succinimides constitute a useful and coveted structural scaffold in organic synthesis, due to their wide range of applications. Herein, we present an efficient approach for the synthesis of substituted succinimides by means of photocatalysis. Namely, tetra n‐butylammonium decatungstate, a well‐known photocatalyst, was employed under light irradiation, in order to synthesize a variety of succinimide ketones via a direct hydroacylation reaction of N‐substituted maleimides. A diverse range of aldehydes and N‐substituted maleimides show great compatibility with this protocol, providing functionalized succinimides in satisfactory yields. The protocol is applicable to flow conditions, as well as in a gram scale reaction.
item.content:encoded:
A photocatalytic protocol to access substituted succinimides via tetra-n-butylammonium decatungstate photocatalysis is reported. This direct radical-driven hydroacylation of a variety of N-substituted maleimides shows tolerance to various aldehydes (or alkanes), affording differentially substituted succinimides in high yields.


Succinimides constitute a useful and coveted structural scaffold in organic synthesis, due to their wide range of applications. Herein, we present an efficient approach for the synthesis of substituted succinimides by means of photocatalysis. Namely, tetra n-butylammonium decatungstate, a well-known photocatalyst, was employed under light irradiation, in order to synthesize a variety of succinimide ketones via a direct hydroacylation reaction of N-substituted maleimides. A diverse range of aldehydes and N-substituted maleimides show great compatibility with this protocol, providing functionalized succinimides in satisfactory yields. The protocol is applicable to flow conditions, as well as in a gram scale reaction.
item.dc:creator: Manos V. G. Lantzanakis, Naya A. Stini, Christoforos G. Kokotos
item.dc:title: Photochemical Decatungstate‐Catalyzed Hydroacylation of Maleimides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Highly Regioselective Ru(II)‐Catalyzed Hydroamination of Ynamides With Phenyl Isocyanates
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 20, 27 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A novel and efficient method for the synthesis of (Z)‐1‐(1‐(2‐oxooxazolidin‐3‐yl)‐2‐phenylvinyl)‐1,3‐diphenylurea derivatives via Ru (II)‐catalyzed hydroamination of ynamides with phenyl isocyanates has been developed.
A novel and efficient method for the synthesis of (Z)‐1‐(1‐(2‐oxooxazolidin‐3‐yl)‐2‐phenylvinyl)−1,3‐diphenylurea derivatives via Ru (II)‐catalyzed hydroamination of ynamides with phenyl isocyanates has been developed. This protocol features excellent regioselectivity with a wide range of substrate scopes of phenyl isocyanates and ynamides. Using this method, highly substituted oxooxazolidinyl phenylvinyl 1,3‐diphenylurea compounds were synthesized in moderate to excellent yields.
item.content:encoded:
A novel and efficient method for the synthesis of (Z)-1-(1-(2-oxooxazolidin-3-yl)-2-phenylvinyl)-1,3-diphenylurea derivatives via Ru (II)-catalyzed hydroamination of ynamides with phenyl isocyanates has been developed.


A novel and efficient method for the synthesis of (Z)-1-(1-(2-oxooxazolidin-3-yl)-2-phenylvinyl)−1,3-diphenylurea derivatives via Ru (II)-catalyzed hydroamination of ynamides with phenyl isocyanates has been developed. This protocol features excellent regioselectivity with a wide range of substrate scopes of phenyl isocyanates and ynamides. Using this method, highly substituted oxooxazolidinyl phenylvinyl 1,3-diphenylurea compounds were synthesized in moderate to excellent yields.
item.dc:creator: Shubham Jaiswal, Amrendra Kumar, Narender Tadigoppula
item.dc:title: Highly Regioselective Ru(II)‐Catalyzed Hydroamination of Ynamides With Phenyl Isocyanates

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Divergent Synthesis of Polycyclic Derivatives via Gold Catalysis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 20, 27 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Gold(I)‐catalyzed cycloisomerization enables the formation of complex 3D structures from simple enyne precursors. This study presents a divergent strategy to synthesize functionalized bicyclic and tricyclic scaffolds from a common alkynyl ketone. Reaction conditions control the pathway (6‐endo‐dig or 5‐exo‐dig), leading to different molecular architectures, including bicyclo and tricyclo derivatives. Mechanistic insights highlight the role of intermediates, solvent, and substituents, offering an efficient route to rigid polycyclic compounds of interest in medicinal chemistry.
Gold(I)‐catalyzed cycloisomerization reactions provide powerful access to structurally complex and 3D molecular frameworks from simple enyne precursors. Herein, we report a divergent gold‐catalyzed strategy for the synthesis of densely functionalized bicyclic and tricyclic polycyclic scaffolds from a common alkynyl ketone platform. Depending on the reaction conditions, solvent, and catalyst, the transformation proceeded through either a 6‐endo‐dig or a 5‐exo‐dig cyclization pathway, leading to distinct molecular architectures. Under selected conditions, bicyclo[3.3.1]nonane derivatives were obtained with high selectivity, while alternative conditions promote skeletal rearrangements delivering bridged tricyclic frameworks, including tricyclo[3.2.1.02,7]octanes and tricyclo[3.3.1.02,8]nonanes. According to literature mechanistic investigations, the involvement and evolution of key vinyl–gold and gold carbene intermediates may govern regioselectivity and product distribution. The role of solvent effects and substituent electronics in controlling pathway divergence was highlighted. This gold‐catalyzed divergent approach enabled rapid molecular complexity generation and provided efficient access to rigid, 3D polycyclic scaffolds of potential interest for medicinal chemistry and drug discovery applications.
item.content:encoded:
Gold(I)-catalyzed cycloisomerization enables the formation of complex 3D structures from simple enyne precursors. This study presents a divergent strategy to synthesize functionalized bicyclic and tricyclic scaffolds from a common alkynyl ketone. Reaction conditions control the pathway (6-endo-dig or 5-exo-dig), leading to different molecular architectures, including bicyclo and tricyclo derivatives. Mechanistic insights highlight the role of intermediates, solvent, and substituents, offering an efficient route to rigid polycyclic compounds of interest in medicinal chemistry.


Gold(I)-catalyzed cycloisomerization reactions provide powerful access to structurally complex and 3D molecular frameworks from simple enyne precursors. Herein, we report a divergent gold-catalyzed strategy for the synthesis of densely functionalized bicyclic and tricyclic polycyclic scaffolds from a common alkynyl ketone platform. Depending on the reaction conditions, solvent, and catalyst, the transformation proceeded through either a 6-endo-dig or a 5-exo-dig cyclization pathway, leading to distinct molecular architectures. Under selected conditions, bicyclo[3.3.1]nonane derivatives were obtained with high selectivity, while alternative conditions promote skeletal rearrangements delivering bridged tricyclic frameworks, including tricyclo[3.2.1.02,7]octanes and tricyclo[3.3.1.02,8]nonanes. According to literature mechanistic investigations, the involvement and evolution of key vinyl–gold and gold carbene intermediates may govern regioselectivity and product distribution. The role of solvent effects and substituent electronics in controlling pathway divergence was highlighted. This gold-catalyzed divergent approach enabled rapid molecular complexity generation and provided efficient access to rigid, 3D polycyclic scaffolds of potential interest for medicinal chemistry and drug discovery applications.
item.dc:creator: Emilie Gentilini, Vincent Davenel, Pierre Ligonès, Jean‐Marie Fourquez, Véronique Michelet
item.dc:title: Divergent Synthesis of Polycyclic Derivatives via Gold Catalysis

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Porcine Liver Esterase‐Catalyzed Dynamic Kinetic Resolution of a Lactone‐Bridged Biaryl: Impact of Organic Cosolvents on Enantioselectivity
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 20, 27 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A stereolabile lactone‐bridged biaryl enables dynamic kinetic resolution to provide a conformationally stable ring‐opened biaryl. A significant positive effect of water‐immiscible organic cosolvents on the enantioselectivity in a reaction catalyzed by porcine liver esterase has been discovered for the first time.
Driven by the growing interest in the synthesis of enantiomerically pure biaryl compounds, we report herein a commercial porcine liver esterase (PLE)‐catalyzed atroposelective ring‐opening reaction of a racemic lactone‐bridged biaryl, providing a biaryl carboxylic acid. A distinctive feature of this method is that the substrate bearing low rotational barrier (ΔG‡rot = 21.9 kcal/mol) undergoes rapid racemization at 30°C, whereas the resulting ring‐opened product is conformationally stable (ΔG‡rot = 30.5 kcal/mol). Consequently, the intrinsic rotational properties of both the substrate and the product enable a dynamic kinetic resolution without the need for an external racemization catalyst. Remarkably, the use of a mixture of a water‐immiscible organic cosolvent, such as cyclopentyl methyl ether, and a buffer solution as reaction medium significantly increased the enantioselectivity of the PLE‐catalyzed reaction to give the biaryl product (>90% yield) with 65% ee. Furthermore, it was concluded that among the several isoenzymes present in the commercial crude PLE, the lactone hydrolysis is primarily catalyzed by its isoenzymes PLE‐5 and PLE‐6.
item.content:encoded:
A stereolabile lactone-bridged biaryl enables dynamic kinetic resolution to provide a conformationally stable ring-opened biaryl. A significant positive effect of water-immiscible organic cosolvents on the enantioselectivity in a reaction catalyzed by porcine liver esterase has been discovered for the first time.


Driven by the growing interest in the synthesis of enantiomerically pure biaryl compounds, we report herein a commercial porcine liver esterase (PLE)-catalyzed atroposelective ring-opening reaction of a racemic lactone-bridged biaryl, providing a biaryl carboxylic acid. A distinctive feature of this method is that the substrate bearing low rotational barrier (ΔG ‡ rot = 21.9 kcal/mol) undergoes rapid racemization at 30°C, whereas the resulting ring-opened product is conformationally stable (ΔG ‡ rot = 30.5 kcal/mol). Consequently, the intrinsic rotational properties of both the substrate and the product enable a dynamic kinetic resolution without the need for an external racemization catalyst. Remarkably, the use of a mixture of a water-immiscible organic cosolvent, such as cyclopentyl methyl ether, and a buffer solution as reaction medium significantly increased the enantioselectivity of the PLE-catalyzed reaction to give the biaryl product (>90% yield) with 65% ee. Furthermore, it was concluded that among the several isoenzymes present in the commercial crude PLE, the lactone hydrolysis is primarily catalyzed by its isoenzymes PLE-5 and PLE-6.
item.dc:creator: Neha Dhiman, Anke Hummel, Kyohei Kanomata, Yoshinari Sawama, Tohru Taniguchi, Harald Gröger, Shuji Akai
item.dc:title: Porcine Liver Esterase‐Catalyzed Dynamic Kinetic Resolution of a Lactone‐Bridged Biaryl: Impact of Organic Cosolvents on Enantioselectivity

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed Perimidine Directed C−H Activation/Annulation for the Synthesis of Hexacyclic Cationic Azaperylenes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 20, 27 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Perimidine has been used as directing group to induce Rh(III)‐catalyzed C−H activation of 2‐aryl‐1H‐perimidines and [4 + 2] annulation with alkynes, leading to hexacyclic cationic azaperylenes A in good to high yields with broad scope. They could also be easily afforded by three‐component triple C−H annulation from all commercial raw materials (naphthalene‐1,8‐diamine, aldehydes, and alkynes). These cationic PAHs show tunable (green to red) luminescence with general > 540 nm emissions.
Annulative π‐extension for the synthesis of cationic azapyrenes has been widely researched, while access to larger π‐systems such as azaperylenes is rare. Herein, perimidine is reported as a fresh directing group to trigger Rh(III)‐catalyzed dual C−H activation/annulation of 2‐aryl perimidines and alkynes, constructing hexacyclic perimidinium salts (benzo‐azaperylenes) A. Salts A could also be synthesized via three‐component triple C−H annulation from commercial naphthalene‐1,8‐diamine, aldehydes, and alkynes. A hexacyclic BF2 complex B1 is conveniently prepared from HO‐substituted perimidine. Three rhodacycles have been detected by ESI‐MS to support the proposed mechanism. The photophysical properties, such as absorptions, emissions, and cyclic voltammetry of these novel cations, are characterized. Salts A show tunable luminescence (516–602 nm) affected by the group from perimidine more than alkyne, and BF2 complex B1 shows emission peaking at 602 nm. The counteranions also have influence on the luminescence, with an order of Cl > BF4 > OTf > SbF6.
item.content:encoded:
Perimidine has been used as directing group to induce Rh(III)-catalyzed C−H activation of 2-aryl-1H-perimidines and [4 + 2] annulation with alkynes, leading to hexacyclic cationic azaperylenes A in good to high yields with broad scope. They could also be easily afforded by three-component triple C−H annulation from all commercial raw materials (naphthalene-1,8-diamine, aldehydes, and alkynes). These cationic PAHs show tunable (green to red) luminescence with general > 540 nm emissions.


Annulative π-extension for the synthesis of cationic azapyrenes has been widely researched, while access to larger π-systems such as azaperylenes is rare. Herein, perimidine is reported as a fresh directing group to trigger Rh(III)-catalyzed dual C−H activation/annulation of 2-aryl perimidines and alkynes, constructing hexacyclic perimidinium salts (benzo-azaperylenes) A. Salts A could also be synthesized via three-component triple C−H annulation from commercial naphthalene-1,8-diamine, aldehydes, and alkynes. A hexacyclic BF2 complex B1 is conveniently prepared from HO-substituted perimidine. Three rhodacycles have been detected by ESI-MS to support the proposed mechanism. The photophysical properties, such as absorptions, emissions, and cyclic voltammetry of these novel cations, are characterized. Salts A show tunable luminescence (516–602 nm) affected by the group from perimidine more than alkyne, and BF2 complex B1 shows emission peaking at 602 nm. The counteranions also have influence on the luminescence, with an order of Cl > BF4 > OTf > SbF6.
item.dc:creator: Hui Li, Haidan Gao, Mingming Yu, Dan Yang, Shiqing Li, Jinhua Wang
item.dc:title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed Perimidine Directed C−H Activation/Annulation for the Synthesis of Hexacyclic Cationic Azaperylenes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Silver‐Catalyzed Three‐Component Radical Aminofluorination of Arylalkenes with Selectfluor and Amino N‐Heterocycles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 20, 27 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A three‐component aminofluorination fuses arylalkenes with Selectfluor and free amino N‐heterocycles, delivering diverse β‐fluoro heterocyclic amines in up to 98% yield.
A silver‐catalyzed three‐component radical aminofluorination of arylalkenes has been developed, utilizing free N‐heterocyclic amines and Selectfluor as key reagents. Under mild conditions, a diverse array of β‐fluoroamine derivatives were obtained in moderate to excellent yields (up to 98%). The protocol exhibits broad substrate scope, tolerating various functional groups such as halogens, alkyls, esters, and cyano groups on both the amino heterocycle and alkene components. Mechanistic studies support a radical pathway involving silver‐mediated oxidation of the amino heterocycle to generate an amino radical, followed by addition to the alkene and subsequent fluorine atom transfer. This method provides a straightforward route to valuable monofluoroalkylated amino N‐heterocycles.
item.content:encoded:
A three-component aminofluorination fuses arylalkenes with Selectfluor and free amino N-heterocycles, delivering diverse β-fluoro heterocyclic amines in up to 98% yield.


A silver-catalyzed three-component radical aminofluorination of arylalkenes has been developed, utilizing free N-heterocyclic amines and Selectfluor as key reagents. Under mild conditions, a diverse array of β-fluoroamine derivatives were obtained in moderate to excellent yields (up to 98%). The protocol exhibits broad substrate scope, tolerating various functional groups such as halogens, alkyls, esters, and cyano groups on both the amino heterocycle and alkene components. Mechanistic studies support a radical pathway involving silver-mediated oxidation of the amino heterocycle to generate an amino radical, followed by addition to the alkene and subsequent fluorine atom transfer. This method provides a straightforward route to valuable monofluoroalkylated amino N-heterocycles.
item.dc:creator: Qiangqiang Liu, Xiao‐Lin Wang, Xiao‐Ming Zhao, Wenyan Dan, Sheng‐Cai Zheng
item.dc:title: Silver‐Catalyzed Three‐Component Radical Aminofluorination of Arylalkenes with Selectfluor and Amino N‐Heterocycles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Hydrogen Peroxide‐Initiated Polarity‐Inversion SNAr of Halophenols with Carboxylic Acids
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 20, 27 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
This report describes a hydrogen peroxide‐initiated SNAr reaction of halophenols with carboxylic acids using aqueous H2O2 as a simple initiator. The method converts halophenols into para‐acyloxy arenes under straightforward conditions, accommodates diverse carboxylic acids, and scales to gram quantities. Electrophile scope depends on ortho substitution adjacent to the phenolic OH, offering a practical carboxylate coupling strategy.
Polarity‐inversion SNAr offers a useful solution to the electronic limitations of classical two‐electron nucleophilic aromatic substitution. Here, we report a hydrogen peroxide‐initiated SNAr of halophenols with carboxylic acids using commercially available 30% aqueous H2O2 as a simple initiator. Under operationally straightforward conditions, this method converts readily available halophenols into para‐acyloxy arenes, accommodates a broad range of carboxylic acids, and is readily scalable to gram scale. The electrophile scope, however, shows a clear dependence on ortho substitution adjacent to the phenolic OH group. Overall, the present method provides a practical and complementary H2O2‐initiated option for thermally driven carboxylate coupling of halophenols.
item.content:encoded:
This report describes a hydrogen peroxide-initiated S N Ar reaction of halophenols with carboxylic acids using aqueous H2O2 as a simple initiator. The method converts halophenols into para-acyloxy arenes under straightforward conditions, accommodates diverse carboxylic acids, and scales to gram quantities. Electrophile scope depends on ortho substitution adjacent to the phenolic OH, offering a practical carboxylate coupling strategy.


Polarity-inversion S N Ar offers a useful solution to the electronic limitations of classical two-electron nucleophilic aromatic substitution. Here, we report a hydrogen peroxide-initiated S N Ar of halophenols with carboxylic acids using commercially available 30% aqueous H2O2 as a simple initiator. Under operationally straightforward conditions, this method converts readily available halophenols into para-acyloxy arenes, accommodates a broad range of carboxylic acids, and is readily scalable to gram scale. The electrophile scope, however, shows a clear dependence on ortho substitution adjacent to the phenolic OH group. Overall, the present method provides a practical and complementary H2O2-initiated option for thermally driven carboxylate coupling of halophenols.

item.dc:title: Hydrogen Peroxide‐Initiated Polarity‐Inversion SNAr of Halophenols with Carboxylic Acids

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper‐Catalyzed, Strain‐Promoted Synthesis of Imidazo[1,2‐f]phenanthridiniums from Cyclic Diaryliodonium Salts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 20, 27 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A highly efficient Cu‐catalyzed method has been developed for constructing valuable imidazo[1,2‐f]phenanthridiniums from readily available imidazoles and torsionally strained cyclic diaryliodoniums. This protocol enables the late‐stage functionalization of imidazole motifs in complex drug molecules.
A highly efficient copper‐catalyzed method has been developed for constructing valuable imidazo[1,2‐f]phenanthridinium derivatives from readily available imidazoles and torsionally strained cyclic diaryliodonium salts. Notably, this protocol is suitable for the late‐stage functionalization of imidazole motifs in complex drug molecules. Mechanistic studies indicate that the reaction proceeds via a tandem strain‐promoted ring‐opening/intramolecular cyclization sequence.
item.content:encoded:
A highly efficient Cu-catalyzed method has been developed for constructing valuable imidazo[1,2-f]phenanthridiniums from readily available imidazoles and torsionally strained cyclic diaryliodoniums. This protocol enables the late-stage functionalization of imidazole motifs in complex drug molecules.


A highly efficient copper-catalyzed method has been developed for constructing valuable imidazo[1,2-f]phenanthridinium derivatives from readily available imidazoles and torsionally strained cyclic diaryliodonium salts. Notably, this protocol is suitable for the late-stage functionalization of imidazole motifs in complex drug molecules. Mechanistic studies indicate that the reaction proceeds via a tandem strain-promoted ring-opening/intramolecular cyclization sequence.
